description: Sync the build_plugin MCP prompt and the plugin-development MCP resource with the build-plugin skill. The skill has evolved to 910 lines with 28 gotchas, XHR interception as a primary technique, a core principle (APIs not DOM), token persistence patterns, adapter injection timing, Trusted Types CSP handling, opaque auth header capture, and silent write failure detection. The prompt and resource are significantly behind — the prompt has only 8 gotchas and no XHR interception, and the resource lacks the core principle and advanced patterns.

userStories.title: Add core principle and XHR interception to build_plugin prompt
userStories.description: The buildPluginPromptText function in mcp-prompts.ts is missing two critical sections from the build-plugin skill: the 'Core Principle: Use Real APIs, Never the DOM' section and the XHR/fetch interception technique (Phase 3, Step 5 in the skill). Add both to the prompt.
acceptanceCriteria: The buildPluginPromptText includes a 'Core Principle' section establishing that every tool must use the web app's own APIs, not DOM scraping
The prompt lists the three acceptable uses of DOM: isReady() auth detection, URL hash navigation, last-resort compose flows
Phase 2 of the prompt includes a new step for XHR/fetch interception with the XMLHttpRequest.prototype monkey-patching pattern
The XHR interception step explains when to use it (apps without clean REST APIs, internal RPC endpoints)
The prompt mentions installing the interceptor at adapter load time to capture auth tokens from early boot requests
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-prompts.ts (modify buildPluginPromptText function, lines 139-485)

Context:
The build-plugin skill (.claude/skills/build-plugin/__SKILL__.md) has two major sections missing from the prompt:

1. "Core Principle: Use the Real APIs, Never the DOM" (skill lines 32-54) — establishes that DOM scraping is never acceptable as a tool implementation. This should be added as an early section in the prompt, before the phase-by-phase workflow. The skill text is comprehensive but the prompt version should be concise (10-15 lines). Key points: every tool uses the same APIs the web app uses, DOM scraping is fragile/limited/slow, when API is hard to discover spend time reverse-engineering (don't fall back to DOM), only three acceptable DOM uses.

2. XHR interception (skill lines 238-290) — a dedicated step for apps without clean REST APIs. The prompt's current Phase 2 has: Step 1 (find tab), Step 2 (network capture), Step 2b (CORS), Step 3 (auth token), Step 4 (test API), Step 5 (auth discovery), Step 6 (map API). The skill adds a new step between auth discovery and API mapping: "Intercept Internal API Traffic" with the XMLHttpRequest.prototype monkey-patching code.

Changes:
1. Add a "Core Principle" section before "Phase 1: Research the Codebase". Keep it to 10-15 lines — extract the key rules from the skill's 22-line section.
2. Add a new Step 5 in Phase 2: "Intercept Internal API Traffic (for apps without clean REST APIs)" between the current Step 4 (test API) and Step 6 (map API surface). Include the XHR interceptor code snippet from the skill. Renumber subsequent steps.
3. The prompt is already 345 lines. Adding ~30-40 lines is fine — the prompt should be comprehensive for AI consumption.

Constraints:
- Do NOT copy the full skill verbatim — the prompt should be concise and action-oriented
- Do NOT remove any existing content — only add new sections
- Keep the existing phase structure intact (Phase 1: Research, Phase 2: Explore, Phase 3: Scaffold, etc.)
- The XHR interceptor code should show the full monkey-patching pattern for XMLHttpRequest.prototype.open/setRequestHeader/send

userStories.title: Add gotchas 9-28 to build_plugin prompt
userStories.description: The buildPluginPromptText Common Gotchas section has only 8 items. The build-plugin skill has 28 gotchas. Add the most important missing gotchas that are relevant to AI agents building plugins via the MCP prompt.
acceptanceCriteria: The Common Gotchas section includes token persistence via globalThis pattern (skill gotcha #4 expanded)
Includes adapter injection timing gotcha (injected at loading + complete)
Includes Trusted Types CSP gotcha (no innerHTML, use regex HTML-to-text)
Includes opaque auth headers gotcha (capture-and-replay with polling wait)
Includes silent write failure gotcha (omit tools that silently fail rather than shipping broken ones)
Includes intercepted headers must survive adapter re-injection gotcha (globalThis persistence for captured tokens)
Total gotchas is between 14-18 (curated selection, not all 28)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-prompts.ts (modify buildPluginPromptText function, lines 475-484)

Context:
The build-plugin skill has 28 gotchas. The prompt should NOT have all 28 — many are too specific (GraphQL query type differences, scaffolder double quotes, Biome formatting). The prompt should include the ones that are universally applicable and that an AI building a plugin via the MCP prompt would actually hit.

Gotchas to ADD (curated from the skill):

9. **Adapter injection timing** — adapters injected at `loading` (before page JS) and `complete` (after full load). isReady() called at both. Cache tokens from localStorage at loading time before host app deletes them. (Skill lines 775-786)

10. **Token persistence on globalThis survives re-injection** — expand existing gotcha #4 with the full pattern: `globalThis.__openTabs.tokenCache.<pluginName>`. Clear on 401. (Skill lines 713-771)

11. **Error classification: parse body before HTTP status** — already gotcha #6, but the prompt version is just one line. Add brief note about JSON error codes in body. (Skill lines 789-812)

12. **Cookie-based auth may require CSRF tokens** — HttpOnly cookies for auth but CSRF token in non-HttpOnly cookie for writes. Check `window.__initialData.csrfCookieName`. (Skill gotcha #21, line 859)

13. **Check bootstrap globals for auth signals** — `window.__initialData`, `window.__INITIAL_STATE__`, `window.boot_data` are more reliable than DOM for auth detection. (Skill gotcha #22, line 860)

14. **Some apps use internal APIs instead of public REST** — public API requires OAuth2, but web client uses internal same-origin endpoints with cookie auth. Look for internal endpoints when public API rejects auth. (Skill gotcha #24, line 862)

15. **Intercepted headers must survive adapter re-injection** — store captured tokens on `globalThis.__<pluginName>CapturedTokens`, re-patch XHR on each adapter load, avoid stale `if (installed) return` guard. (Skill gotcha #25, line 863)

16. **Trusted Types CSP blocks innerHTML** — use regex `html.replace(/<[^>]+>/g, '')` instead. Never use innerHTML/outerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML. (Skill gotcha #26, line 864)

17. **Opaque auth headers can only be captured, not generated** — cryptographic tokens computed by obfuscated JS. Capture from XHR interceptor, implement polling wait with timeout. (Skill gotcha #27, line 865)

18. **Write operations may silently fail without cryptographic tokens** — if a write returns 200 but action doesn't take effect, omit the tool rather than shipping one that silently fails. (Skill gotcha #28, line 866)

That brings the total to 18 gotchas in the prompt.

Constraints:
- Keep each gotcha to 1-2 lines in the prompt (the skill has longer explanations — condense)
- The prompt's gotcha section should remain a numbered list
- Preserve the existing 8 gotchas unchanged — only append new ones
- Do NOT include app-specific gotchas (GraphQL differences, Notion response shapes, scaffolder double quotes)

userStories.title: Add advanced patterns to plugin-development MCP resource
userStories.description: The opentabs://guide/plugin-development resource in mcp-resources.ts is missing the core principle, XHR interception, token persistence via globalThis, adapter injection timing, and cookie-based auth patterns that the build-plugin skill covers.
acceptanceCriteria: Resource includes a 'Core Principle' section stating API-first, no DOM scraping
Resource includes a 'Token Persistence' section with globalThis.__openTabs.tokenCache pattern
Resource includes an 'Advanced Auth Patterns' section covering XHR interception, cookie-based auth with CSRF, and opaque auth header capture
Resource includes an 'Adapter Injection Timing' section explaining loading vs complete injection points
Resource includes a note about Trusted Types CSP and innerHTML alternatives
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-resources.ts (modify PLUGIN_DEVELOPMENT_CONTENT constant, starts at line 292)

Context:
The plugin-development resource is a reference document (~430 lines) covering: architecture, plugin structure, plugin class, defineTool, SDK utilities, ToolError, Zod rules, lifecycle hooks, build/test workflow, common patterns (auth extraction, API wrapper, waiting for state, retrying). It needs to be extended with advanced patterns from the skill.

Add these sections at the end of the existing content (before the closing backtick):

### Core Principle: APIs Not DOM
~5-8 lines. Every tool must use the web app's own APIs. DOM scraping is never acceptable. Only three DOM uses allowed: isReady() auth detection, URL hash navigation, last-resort compose.

### Token Persistence
~15-20 lines. The globalThis.__openTabs.tokenCache.<pluginName> pattern. Why module-level variables reset on extension reload. Code example showing getPersistedToken/setPersistedToken. Clear on 401.

### Adapter Injection Timing
~8-10 lines. Adapters injected at loading and complete. isReady() called at both points. Cache tokens early before host app deletes them.

### Advanced Auth Patterns
~30-40 lines. Three subsections:
1. XHR/fetch interception — monkey-patching XMLHttpRequest.prototype to capture internal API traffic. Brief code snippet.
2. Cookie-based auth with CSRF — HttpOnly cookies for auth, CSRF token from non-HttpOnly cookie for writes.
3. Opaque auth headers — cryptographic tokens that can only be captured and replayed. Polling wait pattern.

### CSP Considerations
~5 lines. Trusted Types blocks innerHTML. Use regex html-to-text. Adapter IIFE bypasses inline script CSP via file-based injection.

This adds ~65-85 lines, bringing the resource to ~500 lines. That's within the 300-400 target range for a dense plugin dev guide (slightly over but justified by the advanced patterns).

Constraints:
- Keep each section concise — this is a reference doc, not a tutorial
- Include TypeScript code examples where the pattern needs exact code (globalThis persistence, XHR interception)
- Do NOT duplicate content already in the resource (e.g., don't repeat the existing auth extraction or API wrapper sections)
- Append new sections after the existing 'Retrying Flaky Operations' section
